source_texts: That meant asking the celebrated doctor regardless of cost. He smiled venomously and said, “No.” She sat for a while, then went over to him and kissed him on the forehead.

The horses had turned to the right, and they started on their preliminary canter, passing in front of the grand stand in a disordered crowd. Then the excited remarks broke out again; every one spoke at the same time.

And the zemstvo official stopped in perplexity, clutching at a tuft of his beard. The lonely domino begged not to be driven out into the Petersburg slush, begged not to be driven out into the malignant fog. The zemstvo official apparently wanted to make a little joke, and grunted: “Mm . . .

K. had just reached the door again when there was a knock. It was the maid, who reported that Fräulein Montag would like a few words with Herr K. and requested that he join her for that purpose in the dining room, where she was awaiting him. K. listened pensively to the maid, then turned with an almost scornful look to a startled Frau Grubach. His look seemed to say he’d long since expected Fräulein Montag’s invitation and that it fit in quite well with the general annoyance he was being forced to suffer at the hands of Frau Grubach’s boarders this Sunday morning. He sent the maid back to say that he would come at once, then went to his wardrobe to change his jacket, responding to Frau Grubach, who was complaining under her breath about the irksome young woman, merely by asking her to please clear away the breakfast dishes. “But you’ve hardly touched anything,” said Frau Grubach. “Oh, just take it away,” cried K; it seemed to him as if Fräulein Montag were somehow mixed up with it all, making it disgusting.

As for the man, he truly was so tired that he did not even make the most of those clean white sheets. He snuffed out his candle with his nostril, the way convicts do, and dropping on to the bed fully dressed, immediately fell into a deep sleep.

“Well, I’d rather put it this way: you and I, we have the same philosophy. That would be a much fairer statement. Je pense, donc je suis, that is something I know for certain. As for the rest—all those worlds, God, and even Satan himself—I’m not sure whether all that exists independently or is merely a passing and subjective emanation of myself .
“I’ll explain everything to you, Olga,” he tried to defend himself. “An important reason forced me to stay away for two weeks. I was afraid.”

“It was all thanks to Kamsa that I was able to enter into this city,” said Vandiya Devan and started recounting yet another adventure of his. Vandiya Devan knew that the city would be on alert and guards would be posted at the gates to capture him. They also knew that he would come bearing the Pazhuvur Signet ring so he couldn’t possibly use it to gain entry.
The Prince produced the twenty-five-rouble note and held it out to Ferdyshchenko. He unfolded it, inspected it, then turned it over and brought it up to the light.

“That’s precisely what he’s missing!” Razumikhin interrupted. “That’s what’s leading you all astray. I maintain that he was inept and inexperienced, and this was probably his first endeavor! If you assume he was a calculating and cunning rogue, then the whole thing turns out improbably. Assume an inexperienced fellow, and it turns out that it was chance alone that saved him from disaster, and what can’t chance do? He might not even have foreseen the obstacles he’d encounter! How did this affair proceed? He took items worth ten or twenty rubles, stuffed them into his pocket, searched around in the old woman’s trunk, among her clothes—but later, in her dresser, in a box in the top drawer, they find fifteen hundred rubles in cash, plus some notes! He didn’t know how to rob; he only knew how to murder! It was his first endeavor, I tell you, his first. He lost his head! It wasn’t calculation, but chance that saved him.”
rationales: Outputs 3 and 4 share distinctive linguistic fingerprints: both use 'celebrated doctor' (instead of 'renowned physician/specialist'), both describe the smile with toxic/poison-related words ('venomously' and 'vitriolic'), both include the detail of her sitting 'for a while' before kissing him on the forehead, and both present the narrative in a similar non-linear order. These specific lexical and structural choices strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the same sentence ordering (crowd reaction first, then horses), both use 'excited remarks' as subject, both note 'everyone/all spoke at once/at the same time', and both describe the horses passing 'in front of the grandstand' with disorder/disorderly phrasing. This structural and lexical consistency strongly indicates they are from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 share the same elaborate sentence structure, including the repeated pleading phrase ('imploring/begging him not to drive him/it from this house back into the Petersburg slush... back into the [cruel/dense] fog'), the detail about grasping a tuft of beard in one hand, the description of the official wanting to make a joke and the joke becoming incoherent. These are clearly produced by the same paraphraser with only minor word-level substitutions (e.g., 'imploring' vs 'begging', 'zemstvo official' vs 'zemstvo member', 'hemmed and hawed' vs 'cleared his throat', 'cruel and dense fog' vs 'dense, malevolent mist').

Outputs 1 and 2 share the same paraphraser. Both follow nearly identical sentence-by-sentence structure, both include K. listening 'pensively' to the maid before casting a near-mocking/disdainful glance at Frau Grubach, both explicitly include the dialogue exchange where Frau Grubach notes K. has barely eaten and K. exclaims to take it away, both mention going to the 'wardrobe' to change, both describe Frau Grubach quietly lamenting/grumbling about the bothersome young woman, and both end with the same concept that Fräulein Montag's involvement made the food nauseating/repulsive. These structural and content parallels are too consistent to be coincidental.

Outputs 0 and 3 share nearly identical phrasing and structure: both use 'extinguished his candle with his nostril,' 'fresh, clean sheets,' 'collapsed onto the bed,' and 'instantly falling into a profound slumber.' The only differences are minor word swaps (e.g., 'utterly exhausted' vs 'so exhausted,' 'barely registered' vs 'didn't even appreciate'), which is consistent with slight variation from the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same paraphrasing style: both remove the French phrase 'Je pense, donc je suis' and replace it with an English equivalent, both use nearly synonymous constructions ('fleeting and personal byproducts of my own thoughts' vs. 'temporary, personal projections of my own mind'), both list 'worlds, God, and even Satan' in parallel, and both employ a concise modern paraphrase style without any meta-commentary or preservation of the original's literary flavor. These linguistic fingerprints strongly suggest the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 1 share the same stylistic fingerprints: identical use of curly em-dash quotation marks, the same dialogue tag phrase 'tried to defend himself,' the same sentence-level structure, and both end with the standalone 'I was afraid.' The only differences are minor reorderings within the same sentence, consistent with the same paraphrasing system generating slight variations.

Outputs 1 and 3 share nearly identical phrasing and structure: both use 'high alert, with guards stationed at the gates,' 'apprehend him,' 'Pazhuvur Signet ring,' 'rendering it useless,' and 'exploits.' They also both restructure the narrative to begin with Kamsa's role and then explain the city alert. These consistent lexical and structural choices strongly suggest they were produced by the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the distinctive phrase 'turned it over' (absent in all others), both split the action into two sentences at the handoff point, and both use a sequential 'looked/studied it, then turned it over and held it to the light' construction. These structural and lexical fingerprints strongly suggest the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 2 share nearly identical structure, sentence progression, and specific details (items worth ten or twenty roubles, fifteen hundred roubles in cash found in a box in the top drawer of the chest, searching the old woman's trunk and clothes, banknotes). Both include the same unique narrative elements absent or altered in other outputs. The minor differences (British 'roubles' vs American 'rubles', single vs double quotes) are superficial, while the underlying paraphrase pattern, vocabulary choices, and content organization are strikingly consistent, pointing to the same paraphrasing system.

human_par3: K. had just reached the door again when there was a knock. It was the maid, who announced that Fräulein Montag would like a word or two with Herr K. and that she accordingly begged him to come to the dining room, where she was waiting for him. K. listened pensively to the message, then he turned an almost mocking eye on the startled Frau Grubach. His look seemed to say that he had long foreseen this invitation of Fräulein Montag's, and that it accorded very well with all the persecution he had had to endure that Sunday morning from Frau Grubach's boarders. He sent the maid back with the information that he would come at once, then went to his wardrobe to change his coat, and in answer to Frau Grubach, who was softly lamenting over the behavior of the importunate Fräulein Montag, had nothing to say but to request her to remove his breakfast tray. "Why, you've scarcely touched anything," said Frau Grubach. " Oh, do take it away," cried K., it seemed to him as if Fräulein Montag were somehow mixed up with the food and made it nauseating.

human_par3: "Well, if you like, I have the same philosophy as you, that would be true. _Je pense, donc je suis_, I know that for a fact; all the rest, all these worlds, God and even Satan--all that is not proved, to my mind. Does all that exist of itself, or is it only an emanation of myself, a logical development of my ego which alone has existed for ever: but I make haste to stop, for I believe you will be jumping up to beat me directly."

human_par3: ‘That’s just the point—it isn’t!’ interrupted Razumikhin. ‘ That’s just what you’re all getting wrong. What I say is that he’s not clever, not experienced, and probably this was his first time! If you imagine it being planned by a clever villain, it doesn’t make sense. But if you imagine someone inexperienced, then it’s quite clear that there was just one lucky chance that saved him from disaster—and chance can do anything. I mean, perhaps he never foresaw any difficulties! And how does he set about it? He takes some things worth ten or twenty roubles apiece and fills his pockets with them, he burrows in the old woman’s trunk, amongst all her rags, and all the while there’s a box in the top drawer of her chest of drawers where they’ve found fifteen hundred roubles in cash, not to mention banknotes! He didn’t even manage to rob her—all he managed to do was kill her! It was his first time , I’m telling you, his first time, and he lost his head! And it wasn’t any planning on his part, it was pure luck that saved him!’
llama_31_8b: Razumikhin cut in, "That's exactly what you're all missing! Your assumptions are leading you down the wrong path.
